humanities_role_explanation: The benchmark uses historical Czech archival documents (content) and is specifically motivated to assist historians and digital humanities researchers in tracing social and political themes across archives (domain).
benchmark_modalities_explanation: Text-only evaluations operating on monolingual transcribed Czech historical document segments obtained via PERO-OCR.
analytical_tasks_explanation: Evaluates document-level identification of semantic topic presence (whether a topic is discussed) and the precise word-level extraction/localization of text spans matching abstract, human-defined topic descriptions.

general_purpose_benchmark.note: Specifically designed as a domain-focused benchmark for topic localization in historical Czech archival documents rather than general-purpose NLP evaluation.

evaluates_generative_llm.note: Evaluates several generative LLMs including GPT-5 variants (gpt-5-2, gpt-5-mini, gpt-5-nano), gpt-oss-20b, Llama 3, Llama 3.2, Gemma 3, and Gemini 3 Pro.

llm_is_primary_system.note: Generative LLMs are evaluated as primary benchmark systems alongside auxiliary fine-tuned BERT baselines.

arts_humanities_data.note: Utilizes Czech historical documents and periodicals, mapping to Language and Linguistics (Czech language parsing, OCR text) and History.

quantitative_metrics.note: Precision, Recall, F1 score, Intersection over Union (IoU), and Krippendorff's alpha for inter-annotator agreement.

published_after_march_2023.note: Published as an arXiv preprint in March 2026.

english_language.note: The paper is written in English, although the underlying evaluated text data is in Czech.

summary: This paper presents CzechTopic, a novel human-annotated benchmark for topic localization in historical Czech documents. Grounded in inter-annotator agreement rather than a single gold reference, models are tasked with identifying and localizing word-level spans that correspond to human-defined, open-ended topic descriptions. The authors establish baselines for both state-of-the-art generative LLMs and specialized BERT-based cross-encoders fine-tuned on distilled data.
key_findings: LLMs demonstrate substantial performance variability, with word-level F1 scores ranging from 13.2 to 61.1. The strongest model (GPT-5-2) achieves a word-level F1 of 61.1, which still lags significantly behind the human baseline (68.7 F1). Specialized BERT-based models fine-tuned on distilled data remain highly competitive, with RobeCzech achieving an F1 of 48.3. Methodologically, matching-based span extraction outperformed tagging by 10.4 F1 points, while prompt language (Czech vs. English) had no significant effect.
review_notes: Ciop labeled primary_evaluation_setting as 'zero-shot', while Cip labeled it 'a mix'. The paper explicitly mentions experiments were conducted in both zero-shot and few-shot settings, so 'a mix' was chosen. Minor phrasing differences in other fields were harmonized.
